claimed_contribution: Introduces an empirical assessment of both short-term and long-term category sales effects of a real assortment reduction in a natural experiment.
Adds a decomposition of sales effects by customer type, distinguishing former buyers versus nonbuyers of delisted items and explicitly analyzing entry of new category buyers.
Combines loyalty-card sales analysis with supplemental process evidence (assortment perceptions and observed search time), and presents cubic-spline modeling for a one-time assortment intervention.
context: The study is a collaborative pilot with a major Dutch grocery retailer in the detergent category. In two test stores, the retailer removed 37 of 150 items (25% of items, 14% of category sales), while two comparable control stores kept their assortments unchanged.
design_and_data: The main analysis uses customer loyalty-card data for 26,941 households across two test and two control stores, covering 52 weeks (26 before and 26 after the reduction) and more than 80% of store sales. Weekly log category sales are modeled with test-control regressions that use cubic spline functions to estimate time-varying post-reduction effects, with controls for promotions and an outlier week. The authors decompose effects by household groups based on pre-reduction behavior: former buyers of delisted items, former buyers of nondelisted items, and new category buyers. Supplemental process data were collected in the two test stores via before/after in-store surveys and observations (n = 333; 179 pre, 154 post) on perceived variety, perceived search efficiency, assortment satisfaction, and observed search time.
main_findings: At the aggregate category level, the 25% item reduction caused a substantial short-term sales decline, while the long-term negative effect was weak and only marginally significant.
Sales losses were concentrated among former buyers of delisted items, whereas former nonbuyers of delisted items showed no significant sales change.

New buyers accounted for a higher sales share in test stores than control stores by the end of the sample (about 38% vs 28%), and supplemental data showed higher perceived search efficiency and lower observed search time after the reduction.
one_line_summary: Using a Dutch grocery natural experiment in detergents, the paper shows that a 25% assortment cut created sizable short-term category sales losses but only weak long-term losses, partly offset by increased buying from new category buyers as search became easier.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior assortment reduction research by separating short-term from long-term sales effects in a natural experiment and by empirically testing whether reduced assortments attract new category buyers. It also complements earlier perception-focused and aggregate-sales studies with supplemental process measures, including observed in-store search time.
research_gap: Prior assortment reduction studies did not distinguish short-term and long-term sales effects, despite the managerial importance of that distinction. The authors also note a lack of empirical evidence on whether assortment reductions attract new category buyers that may offset losses among former buyers of delisted items.
research_question: What are the short-term and long-term effects of a major assortment reduction on total category sales? Do these effects differ between former buyers and former nonbuyers of delisted items, and does the reduction change the share of sales generated by new category buyers?
